planting.note: Plant tubers (bulbs) in spring after soil warms to 65°F or above in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in part shade to full shade; fancy leaf caladiums are among the most spectacular foliage plants for shaded summer gardens in zone 9 and thrive in the hot, humid Gulf Coast summer when planted in shade.

directions: Plant tubers 2 inches deep with the knobby side up in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in part to full shade after soil warms above 65°F; keep consistently moist. Caladium bicolor (fancy leaf caladium) is among the most dazzling shade plants for Gulf Coast gardens — a Brazilian tropical producing enormous, arrow-shaped, paper-thin leaves 6–18 inches long in spectacular combinations of white, pink, red, rose, and green in hundreds of named cultivars, with intricate patterns including bold veining, freckling, splashing, and marbling that make each leaf look individually hand-painted. The leaves are entirely the spectacle — the small flowers that occasionally appear should be removed to direct energy to foliage. Caladiums thrive in the hot, humid shade that challenges most other plants: full shade under live oaks, dappled light under large canopy trees, and shaded patios are ideal. They go dormant and the tubers can be left in the ground in zones 9–12 through mild winters, or dug and stored at 55–60°F. The 'Strap Leaf' (Caladium picturatum) types have narrower leaves; 'Sun Tolerant' cultivars can handle morning sun. A defining seasonal plant for the humid Gulf South shade garden.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Caladiums

planting.note: Plant small tubers in spring after soil warms in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in deep shade to part shade; miniature caladium is the smallest-leaved caladium species with delicate, naturally small foliage and is an excellent choice for container gardens, terrariums, shaded rock gardens, and fine-textured shade border edging.

directions: Plant small tubers 1 inch deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in deep shade; keep consistently moist and protect from any cold. Caladium humboldtii (miniature caladium) is a distinct Venezuelan caladium species producing naturally compact, small-leaved plants reaching only 6–12 inches tall with small (2–4 inch), elegantly proportioned, arrow-shaped leaves in classic white-with-green-veining coloration that is more restrained and subtle than the bold fancy-leaf cultivars. While less commonly grown than Caladium bicolor cultivars, C. humboldtii is valuable for smaller spaces — terrariums, small container arrangements, fine-textured shade borders, and rock garden pockets — where the scale of fancy-leaf caladiums would be overwhelming. It is also more shade-demanding than C. bicolor and thrives in deeper shade conditions. The species has contributed to miniaturized caladium cultivars bred for compact size. In zones below 10, lift tubers before the first frost and store indoors at 55–60°F; they may be replanted the following spring.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Caladiums

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting (seeds are extremely fine — sow on surface without covering); or purchase transplants; plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; wax begonias are among the most reliable flowering shade annuals for Gulf Coast summers and perform well through heat and humidity.

directions: Plant bedding transplants in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in part shade; avoid overwatering, which promotes stem rot. Begonia cucullata (wax begonia or fibrous begonia) is the ubiquitous, versatile bedding begonia sold by the billions as a summer-shade annual — an evergreen South American perennial grown as an annual in temperate gardens. The plants form compact, bushy mounds of glossy, waxy-looking, rounded to shield-shaped leaves in bright green or rich bronzy-chocolate, covered continuously with small, 1-to-1.5-inch, four-petaled flowers in white, pink, rose, red, and salmon from late spring through frost. Wax begonias are remarkable for their shade tolerance combined with genuine heat tolerance — they thrive in conditions that defeat most summer-shade annuals, performing reliably in the high heat and humidity of the Gulf South. The 'Cocktail' and 'Big' series offer bronze-leaved types with outstanding heat tolerance; the 'Super Olympia' series has performed especially well in deep South trials. In zone 9, plants may overwinter if not hit by hard frost. Wax begonia also tolerates more sun than most begonias — the bronze-leaved forms are most sun-tolerant.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bedding Plants
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Annual Flowers

common_name: St. John Wort (Common St. John Wort)

planting.note: Direct sow on surface (seeds need light to germinate) in early spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; St. John's wort is adaptable and easy to establish, self-seeding readily once established — site carefully as it can spread aggressively.

directions: Surface sow seeds in early spring (press lightly into soil but do not cover — seeds require light for germination) in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; thin or transplant to 18 inches. Hypericum perforatum (St. John's wort) is the most important medicinal herb in the Western world after echinacea — a Eurasian perennial producing cheerful, 5-petaled, bright-yellow flowers with numerous golden stamens from June through August, on upright, two-winged, branching stems with small, oval, opposite leaves dotted with translucent oil glands (the characteristic 'perforations' visible when the leaf is held to light). Standardized Hypericum extracts are among the best-documented herbal treatments for mild to moderate depression, with multiple clinical trials showing efficacy comparable to conventional antidepressants. The flowers and upper leaves are harvested at peak bloom and tinctured or dried for medicinal use. The plant is easy to grow in lean, well-drained soils and self-seeds prolifically — it has naturalized across North America and is considered an invasive weed in some western states; grow responsibly. CAUTION: St. John's wort causes photosensitivity in fair-skinned people and may interact with numerous prescription medications including birth control, antidepressants, and anti-rejection drugs.
sources: Herb Society of America — Hypericum perforatum
University of Maryland Medical Center — St. John Wort

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; lance leaf coreopsis is one of the most floriferous and reliable native wildflowers for Gulf Coast and southern gardens and naturalized meadow plantings — it thrives on neglect in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Direct sow seeds in fall or early spring by pressing lightly into well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; deadhead spent flowers to extend bloom season. Coreopsis lanceolata (lance-leaf coreopsis or sand coreopsis) is one of the most abundantly flowering native wildflowers of the Gulf Coastal Plain and eastern North America — a long-lived perennial producing masses of bright, 2-to-3-inch, golden-yellow, daisy-like flowers with serrated ray petals on tall, branching stems above narrow, lance-shaped, mostly basal leaves from May through July (and often reblooming in fall). It is one of four coreopsis species designated as the State Wildflower of Florida and one of the most widely used native perennials in southeastern wildflower meadow restorations, roadside plantings, and naturalistic gardens. Unlike many coreopsis species, C. lanceolata is exceptionally drought-tolerant, self-seeds reliably (but not aggressively), and maintains a neat, upright habit in garden settings. The flower heads are extremely attractive to native bees and small native butterflies. It is one of the most recommended low-maintenance native perennials for full-sun, lean-soil garden situations throughout the Gulf South.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Wildflowers
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Wildflowers
